meta.domain_hint: Generic protocol change rules for structured messages/packets (protocol-agnostic)

changes.change_condition: when HandshakeType is selected in Handshake
changes.change_action: selected from enumerated cases based on HandshakeType
changes.new_value: HelloRequest / ClientHello / ServerHello / HelloVerifyRequest / Certificate / ServerKeyExchange / CertificateRequest / ServerHelloDone / CertificateVerify / ClientKeyExchange / Finished

changes.evidence: case certificate:Certificate;
case certificate_request: CertificateRequest;
case certificate_verify:  CertificateVerify;
case client_hello:  ClientHello;
case client_key_exchange: ClientKeyExchange;
case finished: Finished;
case hello_request: HelloRequest;
case hello_verify_request: HelloVerifyRequest;  // New type
case server_hello:  ServerHello;
case server_hello_done:ServerHelloDone;
case server_key_exchange: ServerKeyExchange;
When responding to a HelloVerifyRequest, the client MUST use the same parameter values (version, random, session_id, cipher_suites, compression_method) as it did in the original ClientHello.
CipherSuite cipher_suites<2..2^16-1>;

changes.change_condition: when the cookie exchange is used during CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.old_value: included in CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.new_value: not included in CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.evidence: the initial ClientHello and HelloVerifyRequest MUST NOT be included in the CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations.
When responding to a HelloVerifyRequest, the client MUST use the same parameter values (version, random, session_id, cipher_suites, compression_method) as it did in the original ClientHello.
changes.note: evidence uses singular 'compression_method' while catalog field is 'compression_methods'

changes.evidence: CompressionMethod compression_methods<1..2^8-1>; } ClientHello;
The client MUST retransmit the ClientHello with the cookie added.

changes.change_condition: Client sends the first ClientHello and does not yet have a cookie
changes.evidence: When sending the first ClientHello, the client does not have a cookie yet; in this case, the Cookie field is left empty (zero length).
opaque cookie<0..2^8-1>;
The server then verifies the cookie and proceeds with the handshake only if it is valid.

changes.change_condition: When the server responds to the client's ClientHello with a HelloVerifyRequest
changes.evidence: This message contains a stateless cookie generated using the technique of [PHOTURIS].
This specification increases the cookie size limit to 255 bytes for greater future flexibility.  The limit remains 32 for previous versions of DTLS.

changes.change_condition: during a limited transition window after Secret changes
changes.evidence: the server can have a limited window during which it accepts both secrets.

changes.change_action: must be present as part of cookie exchange support
changes.new_value: client prepared to do a cookie exchange with every handshake
changes.evidence: Clients MUST be prepared to do a cookie exchange with every handshake.

changes.change_condition: if a server receives a ClientHello with an invalid cookie
changes.new_value: treat it the same as a ClientHello with no cookie
changes.evidence: If a server receives a ClientHello with an invalid cookie, it SHOULD treat it the same as a ClientHello with no cookie.
opaque cookie<0..2^8-1>;                             // New field
opaque cookie<0..2^8-1>; } HelloVerifyRequest;
Cookie = HMAC(Secret, Client-IP, Client-Parameters)

changes.change_condition: when the second ClientHello is received
changes.evidence: When the second ClientHello is received, the server can verify that the Cookie is valid

changes.change_condition: when the server changes the Secret value frequently
changes.evidence: The server can defend against this attack by changing the Secret value frequently, thus invalidating those cookies.

changes.change_condition: each time a ChangeCipherSpec message is sent
changes.evidence: and is incremented each time a ChangeCipherSpec message is sent.
-  A prohibition on epoch wrapping in Section 4.1.
The epoch number is initially zero
A counter value that is incremented on every cipher state change.

changes.change_condition: receipt of application data for the new epoch before the corresponding Finished message for that epoch has been received
changes.new_value: application data packets for the new epoch must be discarded or buffered until the Finished message for that epoch is received
changes.related_state_or_step: application data handling during epoch transition
changes.evidence: Implementations MUST either discard or buffer all application data packets for the new epoch until they have received the Finished message for that epoch.

changes.change_condition: receipt of application data with a new epoch prior to receipt of the corresponding Finished message
changes.new_value: receipt of application data with a new epoch before Finished may be treated as evidence of reordering or packet loss
changes.evidence: Implementations MAY treat receipt of application data with a new epoch prior to receipt of the corresponding Finished message as evidence of reordering or packet loss

changes.change_condition: when a server believes it has an existing association on a given host/port quartet and it receives a ClientHello starting a new handshake
changes.related_state_or_step: detecting a new handshake on an existing host/port quartet
changes.evidence: This will appear to the server as a new handshake with epoch=0.  In cases where a server believes it has an existing association on a given host/port quartet and it receives an epoch=0 ClientHello

changes.new_value: part of the 64-bit value formed by concatenating the epoch and the sequence number in the order they appear on the wire
changes.evidence: the sequence number used to compute the MAC is the 64-bit value formed by concatenating the epoch and the sequence number in the order they appear on the wire

changes.change_condition: when packets are from earlier epochs after a later epoch has begun
changes.change_action: must accept old epoch packets until handshake completes
changes.evidence: Until the handshake has completed, implementations MUST accept packets from the old epoch.

changes.change_condition: within two times the TCP maximum segment lifetime
changes.change_action: must not equal previously used epoch value
changes.evidence: implementations MUST NOT allow the same epoch value to be reused within two times the TCP maximum segment lifetime.

changes.change_condition: if repeated retransmissions do not result in a response, and the PMTU is unknown
changes.new_value: fragmented as appropriate with a smaller record size
changes.evidence: subsequent retransmissions SHOULD back off to a smaller record size, fragmenting the handshake message as appropriate.

changes.change_condition: if the DTLS record layer informs the DTLS handshake layer that a message is too big
changes.new_value: fragmented using any existing information about the PMTU
changes.evidence: If the DTLS record layer informs the DTLS handshake layer that a message is too big, it SHOULD immediately attempt to fragment it, using any existing information about the PMTU.
Hash calculations include entire handshake messages, including DTLS-specific fields: message_seq, fragment_offset, and fragment_length.
Hash calculations include entire handshake messages, including DTLS-specific fields: message_seq, fragment_offset, and fragment_length.

changes.change_condition: when defining the new handshake message hello_verify_request
changes.related_state_or_step: allocation from the TLS HandshakeType registry
changes.evidence: This document defines a new handshake message, hello_verify_request, whose value has been allocated from the TLS HandshakeType registry defined in [TLS12].  The value "3" has been assigned by the IANA.

changes.change_condition: when the cookie exchange is used during CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.old_value: included in CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.new_value: not included in CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations
changes.evidence: the initial ClientHello and HelloVerifyRequest MUST NOT be included in the CertificateVerify or Finished MAC computations.
As in TLS 1.2, the length should not exceed 2^14.

changes.change_condition: Implementations choose to generate an alert for invalid records instead of silently discarding them
changes.evidence: Implementations which choose to generate an alert instead, MUST generate fatal level alerts

changes.change_condition: if a DTLS implementation chooses to generate an alert when it receives a message with an invalid MAC
changes.evidence: it MUST generate a bad_record_mac alert with level fatal
Hash calculations include entire handshake messages, including DTLS-specific fields: message_seq, fragment_offset, and fragment_length.

changes.related_state_or_step: next_receive_seq initialization for received message sequencing
changes.evidence: This counter is initially set to zero.

changes.change_condition: in the case of a rehandshake for HelloRequest
changes.evidence: in the case of a rehandshake, this implies that the HelloRequest will have message_seq = 0

changes.change_condition: in the case of a rehandshake for ServerHello
changes.evidence: the ServerHello will have message_seq = 1.

changes.change_condition: the first message each side transmits in each handshake
changes.evidence: The first message each side transmits in each handshake always has message_seq = 0.

changes.change_condition: when a message is received and its sequence number is greater than next_receive_seq
changes.new_value: implementation SHOULD queue the message but MAY discard it
changes.evidence: If the sequence number is greater than next_receive_seq, the implementation SHOULD queue the message but MAY discard it.

changes.change_condition: when a message is received and its sequence number is less than next_receive_seq
changes.evidence: If the sequence number is less than next_receive_seq, the message MUST be discarded.

changes.change_condition: when a message is received and its sequence number matches next_receive_seq
changes.evidence: When a message is received, if its sequence number matches next_receive_seq, next_receive_seq is incremented and the message is processed.
When a message is retransmitted, the same message_seq value is used.

changes.change_condition: whenever each new message is generated
changes.evidence: Whenever each new message is generated, the message_seq value is incremented by one.
case hello_verify_request: HelloVerifyRequest;  // New type

changes.change_action: must be one of enumerated HandshakeType values
changes.new_value: hello_request(0), client_hello(1), server_hello(2), hello_verify_request(3), certificate(11), server_key_exchange(12), certificate_request(13), server_hello_done(14), certificate_verify(15), client_key_exchange(16), finished(20)
changes.evidence:
enum {
     hello_request(0), client_hello(1), server_hello(2),
     hello_verify_request(3),                          // New field
     certificate(11), server_key_exchange (12),
     certificate_request(13), server_hello_done(14),
     certificate_verify(15), client_key_exchange(16),
     finished(20), (255) } HandshakeType;
When responding to a HelloVerifyRequest, the client MUST use the same parameter values (version, random, session_id, cipher_suites, compression_method) as it did in the original ClientHello.

changes.change_action: copy from client's ClientHello record sequence number
changes.new_value: record sequence number in the ClientHello
changes.evidence: the server MUST use the record sequence number in the ClientHello as the record sequence number in the HelloVerifyRequest.

changes.change_condition: after the initial ServerHello, once the server has created state
changes.evidence: Subsequent ServerHellos will only be sent after the server has created state and MUST increment normally.
Sequence numbers are maintained separately for each epoch, with each sequence_number initially being 0 for each epoch.

changes.change_condition: for each received record during the life of the session
changes.change_action: must not equal any other received record sequence number
changes.evidence: the receiver MUST verify that the record contains a sequence number that does not duplicate the sequence number of any other record received during the life of this session

changes.change_condition: from the perspective of the DTLS record layer, when a retransmission is sent
changes.evidence: This record will have a new DTLSPlaintext.sequence_number value.
The receive window is updated only if the MAC verification succeeds.

changes.change_condition: if a record contains sequence numbers lower than the left edge of the receive window
changes.evidence: Records that contain sequence numbers lower than the "left" edge of the window are rejected.

changes.change_condition: if the received record falls within the window and is new, or if the packet is to the right of the window
changes.related_state_or_step: anti-replay window check before MAC verification
changes.evidence: If the received record falls within the window and is new, or if the packet is to the right of the window, then the receiver proceeds to MAC verification.

changes.change_condition: to avoid sequence number duplication in case of multiple cookie exchanges
changes.change_action: must equal / copied from another field
changes.new_value: record sequence number in the ClientHello
changes.evidence: the server MUST use the record sequence number in the ClientHello as the record sequence number in its initial ServerHello.

changes.new_value: part of the 64-bit value formed by concatenating the epoch and the sequence number in the order they appear on the wire
changes.evidence: the sequence number used to compute the MAC is the 64-bit value formed by concatenating the epoch and the sequence number in the order they appear on the wire
The receiver packet counter for this session MUST be initialized to zero when the session is established.

changes.change_action: must not use for version negotiation judgment
changes.evidence: DTLS 1.2 and 1.0 clients MUST use the version solely to indicate packet formatting (which is the same in both DTLS 1.2 and 1.0) and not as part of version negotiation.

changes.change_action: must equal previously received HelloVerifyRequest server version
changes.evidence: Upon receipt of the ServerHello, the client MUST verify that the server version values match.
DTLS 1.2 server implementations SHOULD use DTLS version 1.0 regardless of the version of TLS that is expected to be negotiated.
The server_version field has the same syntax as in TLS.

changes.change_action: must not assume not-DTLS-1.2 / must not assume eventual negotiation to DTLS 1.0
changes.evidence: DTLS 1.2 clients MUST NOT assume that because the server uses version 1.0 in the HelloVerifyRequest that the server is not DTLS 1.2 or that it will eventually negotiate DTLS 1.0 rather than DTLS 1.2.

changes.change_action: must equal value that would be used in ServerHello
changes.evidence: The server MUST use the same version number in the HelloVerifyRequest that it would use when sending a ServerHello.
When responding to a HelloVerifyRequest, the client MUST use the same parameter values (version, random, session_id, cipher_suites, compression_method) as it did in the original ClientHello.
The only stream cipher described in TLS 1.2 is RC4, which cannot be randomly accessed. RC4 MUST NOT be used with DTLS.

changes.change_condition: for TLS MAC calculation in DTLS
changes.evidence: TLS MAC calculation is parameterized on the protocol version number, which, in the case of DTLS, is the on-the-wire version, i.e., {254, 253} for DTLS 1.2.

changes.change_condition: when employing DTLS version 1.2 in the record
changes.evidence: This document describes DTLS version 1.2, which uses the version { 254, 253 }.
